source_title: Two-year effects of semaglutide in adults with overweight or obesity: the STEP 5 trial
research_question: Do GLP-1 receptor agonists reduce body weight in adults with overweight or obesity?
claim_text: In the STEP 5 trial, once-weekly semaglutide 2.4 mg provided evidence of greater body-weight reduction than placebo at week 104 in adults with overweight or obesity without diabetes.
population: Adults with obesity, or overweight with at least one weight-related comorbidity, without diabetes.
intervention: Once-weekly subcutaneous semaglutide 2.4 mg plus behavioral intervention.
outcome: Percentage change in body weight from baseline to week 104.
result_summary: The paper reports an estimated mean body-weight change from baseline to week 104 of -15.2% with semaglutide versus -2.6% with placebo, with an estimated treatment difference of -12.6 percentage points.

source_span.locator_note: The same result also appears in the abstract on page 1 and Table 2 on page 5 of the local PDF.

short_source_excerpt: body weight from baseline to week 104 was -15.2% ... with semaglutide and -2.6% ... with placebo
limitations: Manual extraction from one paper only.
This record represents one trial result, not a corpus-level conclusion.
Participants were adults without diabetes and trial eligibility criteria limit generalizability.
Both groups received behavioral intervention, so the comparator is not no treatment.
The record does not yet include adverse-event details or discontinuation context.
uncertainty_notes: This evidence bears on the research question, but it does not prove the answer by itself. Later evidence records should preserve trial design, estimand, discontinuation handling, and population constraints.
confidence_note: High confidence that the extracted result is relevant to the research question and source-linked; not a confidence score for the scientific conclusion.

provenance.review_notes: Created as the first manual Evidence Layer prototype. No automated extraction was used.

source_title: Efficacy and safety of semaglutide on weight loss in obese or overweight patients without diabetes: A systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials
research_question: Do GLP-1 receptor agonists reduce body weight in adults with overweight or obesity?
claim_text: Gao et al. synthesized randomized trial evidence and reported that semaglutide was associated with greater body-weight reduction than placebo in adults with obesity or overweight without diabetes.
population: Adults with obesity or overweight without diabetes included in randomized controlled trials of semaglutide.
intervention: Semaglutide at trial-specific anti-obesity doses, including once-weekly 2.4 mg in several included studies.
comparator: Placebo or control groups from included randomized controlled trials.
outcome: Relative and absolute body-weight change from baseline.
result_summary: The meta-analysis reports pooled body-weight changes favoring semaglutide over placebo, including relative body-weight mean difference of -10.09 percentage points and absolute body-weight mean difference of -10.54 kg in the main analysis.

source_span.section: Results / Main analyses / Body weight changes
source_span.locator_note: The body-weight result appears in section 3.4.1 of the local PDF.

short_source_excerpt: Combined statistics showed that semaglutide had significantly greater weight loss than placebo
limitations: Manual extraction from one systematic review/meta-analysis only.
This record summarizes pooled evidence reported by the review authors rather than reanalyzing individual participant data.
Included trials used different semaglutide doses, durations, and study populations.
The paper reports substantial statistical heterogeneity for body-weight outcomes.
This record does not independently assess risk of bias, publication bias, adverse events, or durability after treatment discontinuation.
uncertainty_notes: This evidence bears on the research question by summarizing multiple randomized trials, but it should be interpreted alongside individual trial records, population eligibility, dosing differences, heterogeneity, and safety outcomes.
confidence_note: High confidence that the extracted result is relevant to the research question and source-linked; not a confidence score for the scientific conclusion or corpus-level answer.

provenance.review_notes: Created as the second manual Evidence Layer prototype to test meta-analysis evidence. No automated extraction was used.
